1.blurb: At its peak around 2500 BCE, the Indus Valley Civilization was the largest Bronze Age civilization on earth — its planned cities stretched from the Afghan highlands to the Gujarat coast. By 1900 BCE it was declining, and by 1300 BCE its last cultural markers had faded. The Indo-Aryans arrived into the landscape these city-builders had left behind.

The Hittites in Anatolia were another branch of the Indo-European dispersal from the Pontic steppe — they arrived in what is now Turkey and built an empire that rivaled Egypt, producing the first known Indo-European writing. The Indo-Aryans who entered India were their distant linguistic cousins.

The Mycenaean Greeks were developing chariot-based armies at exactly the same time as the Indo-Aryans — both inherited the spoke-wheeled chariot from the Indo-European steppe tradition. The shaft graves at Mycenae, filled with bronze weapons and chariot gear, are the Greek parallel to the Rigveda's chariot-warrior culture.

The Shang dynasty was building chariot armies at roughly the same time the Indo-Aryans were composing hymns celebrating chariot warfare. Whether the Chinese chariot was independently invented or transmitted from the West via the steppe is debated, but the timing is remarkably close — chariot technology transformed warfare on both sides of Asia simultaneously.

2.blurb: The Battle of Kadesh in 1274 BCE between the Hittites and Ramesses II of Egypt was the largest chariot battle in history — roughly 5,000 chariots on each side. It is roughly contemporary with the Battle of the Ten Kings described in the Rigveda, making both among the oldest battles described in any literature.

3.blurb: The Hittites were working iron as early as 1400 BCE in Anatolia — centuries before widespread iron adoption in India. Their monopoly on iron technology gave them a military edge during the Late Bronze Age, and when the Hittite Empire collapsed around 1180 BCE, iron-working knowledge spread across the ancient world.

China's iron technology developed later than India's but advanced faster — by the 5th century BCE, Chinese foundries were producing cast iron using blast furnaces, a technique Europe would not master for another two thousand years. India was smelting iron earlier, but China industrialized it first.

4.blurb: The same British colonial administration that rigidified the caste system through census classification was also the institution that accidentally discovered the Indus Valley Civilization — when railway workers digging ballast in the 1850s unearthed ancient bricks at Harappa. Colonial archaeology and colonial sociology were reshaping Indian history simultaneously, often in distorting ways.

5.blurb: The pre-Socratic philosophers who emerged in Greek Ionia around 600 BCE — Thales, Anaximander, Heraclitus — were seeking naturalistic explanations for the world at almost exactly the same time as the Upanishadic philosophers in India. Both traditions independently moved from mythological to philosophical thinking during the Axial Age.

Confucius (551-479 BCE) was an almost exact contemporary of the Buddha (roughly 563-483 BCE) — working on the opposite side of Asia, in an entirely different cultural context, with no known contact. Both produced ethical systems that would shape billions of lives. The simultaneity of the Axial Age is hard to ignore.

6.blurb: While India was experiencing its second urbanization around 600 BCE, Mesopotamia had been continuously urban for over three thousand years. Babylon was already an ancient city by the time Varanasi and Rajagriha were being founded — the Ganges cities were newcomers in a world where urban life was old news.

Darius I, who conquered Gandhara around 518 BCE, was the great organizer of the Achaemenid Persian Empire — dividing it into twenty satrapies, building the Royal Road, and introducing a standardized currency. Gandhara became the twentieth satrapy, and Indian soldiers fought in Persian armies as far afield as Greece.

6.matchText: Egypt had been building monumental architecture
6.blurb: Egypt had been building monumental architecture for two and a half millennia by the time India's second urbanization began around 600 BCE. The New Kingdom pharaohs ruled an empire stretching from Nubia to Syria — making India's cityless millennium between the Harappan decline and the Gangetic cities all the more striking in comparison.

7.blurb: The pipal tree under which the Buddha achieved enlightenment was the same species that appears prominently on Harappan seals two thousand years earlier — one of the most vivid examples of how Harappan religious elements persisted through the Vedic period and into Buddhism, surviving the collapse of the civilization that first venerated them.

8.matchText: Macedonian king who had already conquered the entire Persian Empire
8.blurb: Alexander had already destroyed the Achaemenid Persian Empire — the largest empire the world had yet seen — before he turned east toward India. His defeat of Darius III at Gaugamela in 331 BCE ended two centuries of Persian rule from Egypt to Central Asia, and it was the Persian imperial roads that carried Alexander to the Hindu Kush.

Hammurabi's famous law code, carved on an eight-foot diorite pillar around 1750 BCE, was Mesopotamia's first attempt to convert customary practice into written law — over a thousand years before the Indian Dharmasutras attempted the same task. Both represent their civilization's effort to create a systematic legal framework, though Hammurabi's code is state law while the Dharmasutras are religious prescriptions.

The Indus script, used across the Harappan civilization for over six centuries, remains undeciphered — and when it died out around 1700 BCE, the subcontinent entered a millennium-long writing gap. The Brahmi script that finally appeared around the 3rd century BCE has no demonstrable connection to the Indus script, making India unique among ancient civilizations in having two completely separate writing traditions.
